era_context: Late Bronze Age, reign of Amenhotep III in Egypt. The New Kingdom was at its zenith of power and cultural achievement. The Hittite Empire under Šuppiluliuma I was expanding in Syria and Mesopotamia. Mycenaean Greece was in the Late Helladic II period, with palace centers at Mycenae, Tiryns, and Pylos. The Shang Dynasty continued in China; Kassite Babylonia was stable under Burnaburiash II.

events.title: Amenhotep III's reign; continued temple construction and diplomatic activity
events.description: Amenhotep III's reign continued as a period of unprecedented Egyptian prosperity and artistic achievement. Temple construction, including work on the Luxor Temple and the Colossi of Memnon, proceeded. The pharaoh maintained extensive diplomatic correspondence with foreign powers and consolidated Egypt's position as a superpower in the ancient Near East.
events.certainty_note: Amenhotep III's reign is well-documented, but specific events within individual years are rarely dated precisely.

events.title: Šuppiluliuma I consolidates Hittite control in Syria
events.description: Šuppiluliuma I (r. c. 1370–1330 BCE) was engaged in military campaigns to expand and consolidate Hittite control over northern Syria and Mesopotamia. His campaigns against Mitanni and Amorite states were part of a broader strategy to establish Hittite hegemony in the region. These military activities are documented in Hittite royal inscriptions and are reflected in the Amarna Letters, which discuss the shifting balance of power.
events.certainty_note: Šuppiluliuma I's military campaigns are well-attested, but specific years are difficult to pinpoint. This event is assigned to this period based on his reign dates and historical context.

disconfirming_evidence: The chronology of Šuppiluliuma I's reign is debated. Some scholars place his reign c. 1370–1330 BCE, while others propose c. 1345–1315 BCE. The dating of his military campaigns is therefore uncertain. No disconfirming evidence contradicts the events listed, but the precise chronological placement is contested.
historiographic_note: The historical record for this period is moderate. Egyptian sources are relatively abundant, but the Hittite record is less detailed for specific years. The Amarna Letters provide crucial context for international relations but are undated. The chronology of the Late Bronze Age remains one of the most contested issues in ancient history, with competing chronologies differing by up to 100 years. This affects the precise dating of all events in this period.

graph_edges.note: Hittite expansion continued and eventually led to conflict with Egypt
